pages.title: Welcome to Multi-Factor Authentication
pages.subtitle: Learn how multi-factor authentication helps protect accounts even if a password is stolen.

pages.title: What is multi-factor authentication?
pages.text: Multi-factor authentication, or MFA, is a security measure that requires more than one form of verification before allowing access to an account. Instead of relying only on a password, MFA adds another layer of protection. This helps reduce the risk of unauthorized access if a password is stolen, guessed, or reused.

pages.title: Why MFA matters
pages.text: Passwords alone are not always enough to protect an account. A password may be exposed through phishing, password reuse, data breaches, or social engineering. MFA helps stop attackers from logging in even if they know your password.

pages.title: Common MFA methods
pages.text:
Common forms of MFA include:
  • Authenticator apps that generate temporary login codes
  • Push notifications asking you to approve a login
  • Hardware security keys that plug into a computer or phone
  • Text message (SMS) codes sent to a phone
Examples of commonly used authenticator apps include:
  • Microsoft Authenticator
  • Google Authenticator
  • Duo Mobile
  • Okta Verify
These apps generate short codes that change every 30 seconds and are used along with your password during login.

pages.title: Stronger MFA methods
pages.text:
Some MFA methods provide stronger protection than others.
  • Authenticator apps are generally safer than text messages because they do not rely on phone networks.
  • Hardware security keys provide one of the strongest forms of MFA protection.
Text message codes are still helpful, but they can sometimes be intercepted through phone number attacks or SIM swapping. Whenever possible, organizations should prefer authenticator apps or hardware security keys.

pages.title: MFA adds protection beyond passwords
pages.text:
A strong password is still important, but MFA provides a second layer of defense.

If an attacker steals a password, MFA can help stop them from accessing the account unless they also have the second factor.

This is why organizations often require MFA for email, remote access, and sensitive systems.

pages.title: Unexpected MFA prompts
pages.text: If you receive an MFA prompt that you did not expect, it may be a warning sign. An attacker could be trying to log in with your password and hoping you approve the request by mistake. Do not approve unexpected login requests. Unexpected MFA prompts should be treated seriously and reported according to your organization’s process.

pages.title: Push fatigue attacks
pages.text: A push fatigue attack happens when an attacker repeatedly triggers MFA approval requests, hoping the user becomes annoyed, confused, or accidentally approves one. This is a form of social engineering. Repeated unexpected prompts are not normal and should never be approved just to make them stop.

pages.title: Never share MFA codes
pages.text: MFA codes are meant to verify your identity. You should never share MFA codes, approval prompts, or authentication requests with another person, even if they claim to be from IT, a bank, or another trusted organization. Legitimate support staff should not ask you to provide your MFA code for routine access.

pages.title: What to do if something seems wrong
pages.text:
If you receive an unexpected MFA prompt or think your account may be under attack:
  1. Do not approve the request
  2. Change your password if needed
  3. Report the incident using your organization’s process
  4. Review your account activity if available
Quick action helps reduce the risk of unauthorized access.

image.alt: Safe response to suspicious MFA activity
